message.content:
Research Unix/Linux file naming conventions and patterns for configuration files.

Focus on:
1. Dotfile patterns:
   - .file (hidden)
   - .filerc (run commands)
   - .file.d/ (directory of configs)
   - file.conf
   - file.cfg

2. Special directory patterns:
   - /etc/X.d/ (modular configs)
   - ~/.config/X/ (XDG standard)
   - .local/ hierarchy

3. Semantic naming patterns:
   - MANIFEST
   - Makefile
   - Dockerfile
   - Vagrantfile
   - Procfile
   - CODEOWNERS
   - CLAUDE.md

4. What pattern would be most semantic for "agent rules"?
   - .agentrc
   - .agent/rules
   - AGENTRULES
   - agent.rules
   - .rules
   - __rules__ (Python dunder style)

Return analysis of which patterns are most recognized and semantic.

content.text: Vou pesquisar as convenções de nomenclatura de arquivos Unix/Linux para configurações.

input.query: Unix Linux dotfile naming conventions .rc .conf .d directory patterns 2025

semantic file naming patterns Makefile Dockerfile CODEOWNERS conventions

Here are the search results for Unix/Linux dotfile naming conventions:

## Summary of Findings

### Dotfiles Overview
Dot files (or "hidden files") are configuration files in Unix-like systems whose names start with a dot (.). This naming convention hides them by default from standard ls commands (you'll need ls -a to list them).(no content)They live in your home directory (~) and control the behavior of applications, shells, and system tools.

### The ".rc" Suffix Origin
When Unix was developed in the 1970s, its creators (Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie) borrowed the "run commands" idea. The system's startup script was named /etc/rc (still used today in some Linux distros), short for "run commands." Over time, this convention trickled down to user-specific configuration files like .bashrc and .vimrc.(no content)A common misconception is that 'rc' stands for "Resource Configuration" or "Runtime Configuration." While these descriptions fit what rc files do (they configure resources or runtime behavior), they are not the original origin. This is a retrospective reinterpretation of the acronym.(no content)Verdict: 'rc' most likely derives from "Run Commands", honoring its CTSS/Unix heritage.

### The ".d" Directory Pattern
Avoid monolithic rc files by splitting settings into smaller files in a .d directory. This is a common convention for modular configuration (e.g., `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/`, `/etc/sudoers.d/`).

### Common Dotfile Examples
Dotfiles are used to define shell aliases (e.g., alias ll='ls -la'), set environment variables (e.g., PATH, PS1 for the prompt), configure editors (e.g., Vim keybindings in .vimrc), and manage tool-specific settings (e.g., Git in .gitconfig).

### File Naming Rules
File names may NOT contain: a NULL character, or a / (forward slash). A file may not be named . or .. as these are reserved names.(no content)Certain characters have special meaning to the operating system and should be avoided: \ " ' * ; - ? [ ] ( ) ~ and others.

### Best Practices
Store dotfiles in a Git repository (e.g., ~/.dotfiles) to track changes, revert mistakes, and sync across machines.(no content)Remove unused settings, and avoid overloading them with scripts better suited for separate tools (e.g., use cron for scheduled tasks instead of .bashrc).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "semantic file naming patterns Makefile Dockerfile CODEOWNERS conventions"

Links: [{"title":"Docker Style Guide - Broad Institute Long Read Pipelines","url":"https://broadinstitute.github.io/long-read-pipelines/development_guide/docker_style_guide/"},{"title":"File Naming Conventions","url":"https://yourfilenameiswrong.com/"},{"title":"Best Practices for Writing Dockerfiles.","url":"https://www.xcubelabs.com/blog/best-practices-for-writing-dockerfiles/"},{"title":"Dockerfile naming convention · Issue #15549 · moby/moby","url":"https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/15549"},{"title":"Use <purpose>.dockerfile naming convention · Issue #9245 · docker/docs","url":"https://github.com/docker/docker.github.io/issues/9245"},{"title":"The Complete Guide to Setting Image Names in Dockerfiles – TheLinuxCode","url":"https://thelinuxcode.com/set-image-name-dockerfile/"},{"title":"Dockerfile Naming Convention and Organization – mohitgoyal.co","url":"https://mohitgoyal.co/2018/08/29/dockerfile-naming-convention-and-organization/"},{"title":"Ten simple rules for writing Dockerfiles for reproducible data science | PLOS Computational Biology","url":"https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1008316"},{"title":"Makefile Conventions (GNU Coding Standards)","url":"https://www.gnu.org/prep/standards/html_node/Makefile-Conventions.html"},{"title":"Codemia | Master System Design Interviews Through Active Practice","url":"https://codemia.io/knowledge-hub/path/how_to_name_dockerfiles"}]

Here are the search results for semantic file naming patterns for Makefile, Dockerfile, CODEOWNERS, and conventions:

## Dockerfile Naming Conventions

Adopt consistent naming conventions for Dockerfiles, making it easy for developers to locate the appropriate file for a specific service or component. Consider using a standardized prefix or suffix to distinguish Dockerfiles based on context.(no content)The `.dockerfile` extension already works for syntax highlighting in VS Code using the glob pattern. Also, using a lowercase `.dockerfile` would match with general naming convention for other extensions. It's recommended to avoid capitalising `.Dockerfile` but keep `Dockerfile` for extensionless naming (e.g., in the case of only ever requiring the use of one Dockerfile for your project).(no content)Some advocate to adapt documentation to promote the use of `<purpose>.dockerfile` naming convention.(no content)When there are a lot of dockerfiles in a project, the "How do I name them" question arises. The Docker community needs to come up with a good convention, something better than "Dockerfile". One suggestion is to use the `.dockerfile` extension.

## File Extension Placement

Many developers and projects unfortunately use inconsistent file naming patterns, especially for configuration files. We often see patterns like `.env.development` instead of `development.env`. `.env`, `.dockerfile` and others are actually file extensions, and this creates confusion and inconsistency in codebases.(no content)File extensions tell us what type of file we're looking at. The name before the extension should tell us its purpose or variant, not the other way around.

## Makefile Conventions

The GNU Coding Standards describe conventions for writing the Makefiles for GNU programs. Using Automake will help you write a Makefile that follows these conventions.(no content)It is good practice to provide a Makefile alongside your Dockerfile, which can capture the specific commands.(no content)A standard project structure includes a Makefile for building the Docker image, along with optional files like README.md for documentation.

## Semantic Versioning in Docker

Use semantic versioning: Follow the semantic versioning pattern (major.minor.patch) to ensure consistency and compatibility between different versions of the image. Avoid special characters in the image name or tag, as it may cause issues with some systems or platforms.(no content)Images have tags associated with them, and these tags have specific meanings, e.g., a semantic version indicator such as `3.7` or `dev`, or variants like `slim` that attempt to reduce image size. Tags are defined at the time of image build and appear in the image name after the `:`. By convention a missing tag is assumed to be the word `latest`.

The search didn't return specific results about CODEOWNERS conventions. Would you like me to perform an additional search specifically for CODEOWNERS file naming conventions?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "XDG Base Directory Specification config file naming conventions"

Links: [{"title":"XDG Base Directory - ArchWiki","url":"https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/XDG_Base_Directory"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification","url":"https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification | Alchemists","url":"https://alchemists.io/articles/xdg_base_directory_specification"},{"title":"Use the XDG Base Directory Specification!","url":"https://xdgbasedirectoryspecification.com/"},{"title":"XDGBaseDirectorySpecification - Debian Wiki","url":"https://wiki.debian.org/XDGBaseDirectorySpecification"},{"title":"XDG/Base Directories - Gentoo wiki","url":"https://wiki.gentoo.org/wiki/XDG/Base_Directories"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification","url":"https://developer-old.gnome.org/basedir-spec/"},{"title":"XDG cheat sheet · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/roalcantara/107ba66dfa3b9d023ac9329e639bc58c"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory :: IT'S FOSS","url":"https://itsfoss.gitlab.io/blog/xdg-base-directory/"},{"title":"Base Directories — PyXDG 0.26 documentation","url":"https://pyxdg.readthedocs.io/en/latest/basedirectory.html"}]

Here's information about the XDG Base Directory Specification and config file naming conventions:

## XDG Base Directory Specification Overview

The XDG Base Directory Specification defines an organized folder and file structure for applications to store associated user configuration, cache, data, state, and runtime information on UNIX-like systems. This allows for consistency across different programs and desktops.

### Key Environment Variables and Default Directories

| Variable | Purpose | Default |
|----------|---------|---------|
| $XDG_CONFIG_HOME | User-specific configuration files | $HOME/.config/ |
| $XDG_DATA_HOME | User-specific data files | $HOME/.local/share/ |
| $XDG_STATE_HOME | User-specific state files | $HOME/.local/state/ |
| $XDG_CACHE_HOME | User-specific non-essential data files (cache) | $HOME/.cache |
| $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR | User-specific non-essential runtime files (sockets, named pipes, etc.) | Set by system |

### Config File Naming Conventions

Application-specific configuration files are typically located within subdirectories of this directory, following the application's name or a similar identifier.(no content)For instance, the configuration file for the text editor nano might be found at ~/.config/nano/config.

According to the specification:

Specifications may reference this specification by specifying the location of a configuration file as $XDG_CONFIG_DIRS/subdir/filename. This implies that default configuration files should be installed to $sysconfdir/xdg/subdir/filename with $sysconfdir defaulting to /etc. A user specific version of the configuration file may be created in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/subdir/filename.

### Best Practices

Keep the XDG .config folder organized so each application is clearly identified. This also applies for .cache, .local, and any runtime information you might have associated with your application.(no content)Each application can manage its own sub-folder (i.e., namespace) so the application can manage multiple files and/or additional sub-folders as desired.(no content)The configuration doesn't have to be YAML but could be INI, TOML, or whatever format best suits the needs of the application.(no content)When saving config paths, the 'resource' should normally be the name of your application.

### Path Priority

When the same information is defined in multiple places, the information defined relative to the more important base directory takes precedence. The base directory defined by $XDG_DATA_HOME is considered more important than any of the base directories defined by $XDG_DATA_DIRS. The base directory defined by $XDG_CONFIG_HOME is considered more important than any of the base directories defined by $XDG_CONFIG_DIRS.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Single and Double Underscores in Python Names – Real Python","url":"https://realpython.com/python-double-underscore/"},{"title":"The Meaning of Underscores in Python – dbader.org","url":"https://dbader.org/blog/meaning-of-underscores-in-python"},{"title":"PEP 8 – Style Guide for Python Code | peps.python.org","url":"https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/"},{"title":"Underscores, dunders and everything nice","url":"https://www.hacksoft.io/blog/underscores-dunders-and-everything-nice"},{"title":"Dunder (Double UNDERscore) Alias","url":"https://wiki.python.org/moin/DunderAlias"},{"title":"Getting started with classes in Python Part 8: \"dunder\" | Ansys Developer Portal","url":"https://developer.ansys.com/blog/getting-started-classes-python-part-8-dunder"},{"title":"What does double underscore prefix do in Python variables?","url":"https://www.tutorialspoint.com/what-does-double-underscore-prefix-do-in-python-variables"},{"title":"Single and Double Underscores in Python - GeeksforGeeks","url":"https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/python/single-aad-double-underscores-in-python/"},{"title":"Python and the underscore (_) | Towards Data Science","url":"https://towardsdatascience.com/python-and-the-underscore-82d7ce8706d/"},{"title":"Single and Double Underscores in Python explained | by Leendert Coenen | Python explainers | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/python-explainers/single-and-double-underscores-in-python-explained-63a805ef34db"}]

Here's what I found about Python's dunder (double underscore) naming conventions:

## What is "Dunder"?

"Dunder" is short for "double underscore" - a term the Python community uses because double underscores appear frequently in Python code. For example, you'd pronounce `__baz` as "dunder baz".(no content)The term was proposed as a way to pronounce these names: "double underscore should be pronounced 'dunder'. So `__init__` is 'dunder init dunder', or just 'dunder init'."

## Types of Underscore Naming Patterns

### 1. Double Leading and Trailing Underscores (`__name__`)
Double leading and trailing underscores—known as dunders—denote special methods or attributes, such as `.__init__()`, `.__len__()`, and `__name__`, which Python uses to support internal behaviors.(no content)`__double_leading_and_trailing_underscore__`: "magic" objects or attributes that live in user-controlled namespaces. E.g. `__init__`, `__import__` or `__file__`. Never invent such names; only use them as documented.

### 2. The `__name__` and `__main__` Pattern
When code is run as a script, `__name__` is set to `'__main__'`, which is the name of the top-level environment of the program. When code is imported as a module, `__name__` is set to the name of the module file.(no content)This name-main idiom allows you to execute code when you run the Python file as a script, but not when you import it as a module.

### 3. Double Leading Underscores (`__attribute`)

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the origin and naming conventions of uppercase file names like Gemfile, Procfile, and Makefile.

## Origin of Uppercase "Filefile" Naming Convention

The search results reveal that these capitalized file names follow what's often called the "Filefile" naming convention:

"At the time when Bundler came out, the so called 'Filefiles' were quite popular. There's the Procfile introduced by Heroku and foreman, we all know Rakefile and of course, all of this was probably introduced by the mighty Makefile."

### The Pattern's Origin

The convention traces back to **Makefile**, which was the original inspiration for this naming pattern. When Bundler was created for Ruby, it followed this established convention with **Gemfile**, and Heroku extended it with **Procfile** for process management.

### Criticism of the Convention

Critics have raised several arguments against this naming scheme: "The capitalization is odd and uncommon in the rest of the world of programming. The 'file' part is superfluous, it's a bit like if Rails' app folder would be called Appfolder. That would be weird, huh? The fact that none of these have any extension makes it kind of hard to tell what's actually in the file (hard to tell for humans, but also for editors, for example)."

### Modern Alternatives

Bundler has introduced alternative naming with `gems.rb`: "The name is all lowercase, the purpose is clearly stated in the name and a proper Ruby file extension makes it easy for editors to pick the right syntax highlighting and it is clear from the beginning that the contents will be Ruby code of sorts."

Based on my search results, here's what I found about CODEOWNERS file naming conventions for GitHub and GitLab:

## File Naming Convention

The file must be named `CODEOWNERS` (in all uppercase). On GitHub, you should create a new file called CODEOWNERS in the `.github/`, root, or `docs/` directory of the repository.(no content)If CODEOWNERS files exist in more than one of those locations, GitHub will search for them in that order and use the first one it finds.

## GitLab Locations

GitLab checks these locations in your repository in this order. The first CODEOWNERS file found is used, and all others are ignored: In the root directory: `./CODEOWNERS`.(no content)In the docs directory: `./docs/CODEOWNERS`. In the .gitlab directory: `./.gitlab/CODEOWNERS`.

## Key Points

- **The filename must be uppercase**: `CODEOWNERS` (not `codeowners` or `CodeOwners`)
-
